close_reason: Added glibc DNS error string. Full reqwest downcast requires adding reqwest as direct dep; keeping substring approach with improved coverage for now.
description: src/lib.rs:211-235 classifies SDK errors into transient/permanent by substring-matching e.to_string(): "[5", "[429]", "connection refused", "connection reset", "timed out", "dns error", "No such host", "error sending request". Risks: (1) reqwest's error message text is not part of its public API and varies across reqwest versions and platforms — Linux's getaddrinfo returns 'Name or service not known' on glibc and 'No address associated with hostname' on others; the match for 'No such host' is Windows-flavoured. DNS failures on glibc Linux will fall through to Backend (permanent) instead of Unavailable (transient). (2) The substring '[5' will match ANY occurrence in the chain — e.g. an error containing 'status=[503]' from a layer above will be classified transient even if the underlying cause is a permanent 4xx wrapped with a 5xx-flavoured outer message. (3) bitwarden-core's display format for ResponseContent is part of its private API; an upgrade can silently change it. The code already documents this risk (lib.rs:194-210). Recommended fix: in addition to substring matching, walk the source chain via std::error::Error::source() and downcast to known concrete types (reqwest::Error has is_timeout(), is_connect(), is_request(); std::io::ErrorKind has Connection*). Even imperfect downcasting is more robust than text matching. At minimum, add unit tests with synthetic errors covering each substring branch so a future SDK upgrade that changes one of those strings fails CI loudly.
title: classify_bitwarden_sdk_error matches on unstable substrings of e.to_string()
